channel.title: Fire (Ice fork) — Updates
channel.description: Sparkle update feed for the Fire maintained fork of jordanbaird/Ice. Releases published here flow into the in-app "Check for Updates" dialog.

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.8
item.description:
fire.8 — AI can now move menu bar items

First fire build where AI assistants can actually rearrange the menu bar, not just read it.

What's new

  • Real write ops via MCP: move_item, hide_item, show_item, apply_layout now post real CGEvent drags. AI moves items between sections like you would by hand.
  • New MCPBackend.xpc service handles writes; existing MenuBarItemService.xpc stays for sourcePID lookups.
  • Lean Mover.swift (~530 lines): faithful port of upstream Ice's CGEvent drag pipeline. Same three-EventTap synchronization dance, decoupled from AppState.
  • SourcePIDCache shared across XPC services so macOS 26 Control Center reparenting resolves correctly (no more bundle IDs collapsing to com.apple.controlcenter).

From fire.7.1 (included)

  • list_layouts MCP tool — AI can ask "what layouts has the user saved?" without trial-and-error.

From fire.7 (included)

  • Multi-display support in list_items — iterates all active displays, picks the right Ice control items per display.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.8.1
item.description:
fire.8.1 — moves now work on macOS 26

Hotfix on top of fire.8. The wire plumbing shipped in fire.8 was correct, but a live smoke test through Claude Code uncovered two bugs that kept moves from actually landing on macOS 26.

Fixes

  • Real bundle IDs in list_items: SourcePIDCache was never started in MCPBackend.xpc, so every item resolved as com.apple.controlcenter on macOS 26 Control Center reparenting. Now resolved correctly.
  • Section detection works: Ice's three control items live in NSStatusBar and are NOT separate CG windows, so the section algorithm always fell through to "everything is alwaysVisible". Ice main app now publishes its section-divider X positions to its plist, and MCPBackend reads them as section boundaries.
  • Apple widgets stay Apple's: Control Center widgets (BentoBox, Clock, FaceTime, MusicRecognition, AudioVideoModule) are now identified by their stable window titles, so moves that land them next to Ice's section divider still classify correctly.
  • XPC service displays: CGGetActiveDisplayList sometimes returns zero displays from an XPC service with no graphics connection; falls back to the main display so list/move still work.

Live verified: a Claude Code session ran list_items (returned 21 real items grouped by section), then move_item bundle_id=com.electron.ollama to_section=alwaysVisible physically slid the item to the rightmost slot — confirmed by the next list_items showing ollama at pos=19.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.8.4
item.description:
fire.8.4 — AI can hide menu bar items into any section

The big one: AI assistants can now move items into the Hidden and Always-Hidden sections, not just rearrange the visible row. hide_item, show_item, move_item, and apply_layout all work across every section.

What changed

  • Ice-performs-writes bridge: moving an item into a collapsed/empty section can't be done from the MCP helper process by coordinate dragging (the section's divider is parked off-screen). Write operations are now delegated to the Ice app itself via a local file channel, so they use the exact code path Ice's own Layout editor uses — which reaches every section.
  • Off-screen dividers found correctly: control items are located via the same active-space enumeration Ice's editor uses, so an empty Hidden section is a valid drop target.

Live verified end-to-end via Claude Code: hide_item moved Ollama from the visible row into the (empty) Hidden section — it vanished from the menu bar — and show_item brought it straight back.

Local-only: writes go through a file on your Mac, never the network.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.9
item.description:
fire.9 — AI Quotas in the menu bar

Fire is a menu-bar manager for AI-heavy Macs. fire.9 adds AI Quotas: an optional menu-bar readout of how much usage you have left across Codex, Claude, Gemini, and Ollama, plus reset times.

Combined menu-bar item, e.g.:

AI Cx99 Cl81 Gm? Ol100

The dropdown breaks each provider down into session and weekly windows with reset times. A provider that can't be read shows ?.

Details

  • Off by default. Enable it in Settings → Advanced → AI Quotas.
  • Local-only. Usage is read via your local CodexBar CLI installation; quota data never leaves your Mac (not sent to Sentry or MCP).
  • One menu-bar item with a stable position; refreshes every 5 minutes, or on demand from the dropdown.
  • Needs the CodexBar CLI installed (auto-detected, or set a custom path in settings).

Also includes the fire.8.x menu-bar write bridge: AI assistants can move and hide items across every section via MCP.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.9.2
item.description:
fire.9.2 — AI Quotas adds Antigravity

AI Quotas now tracks Antigravity (Google's Gemini-backed coding tool) alongside Codex, Claude, Gemini, and Ollama. The combined item reads e.g.:

AI Cx99 Cl73 Gm? Ag100 Ol100

The dropdown breaks Antigravity down by model (Gemini 2.5/3 Pro, Flash, etc.).

  • New providers added in an update now appear automatically (provider selection persists the disabled set, so the default stays "all on").
  • Still off by default, still local-only.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.9.4
item.description:
fire.9.4 — AI Quotas now actually shows in the menu bar

Fixes a real architectural bug: the AI Quotas readout could end up invisible (parked off-screen) because Ice is a menu-bar manager and was treating its own readout like a third-party item to hide.

Rebuilt the AI Quotas item as a first-class, always-visible Ice element (same mechanism Ice uses to keep its own icon visible). It now sits reliably in the visible part of the menu bar: AI Cx99 Cl73 Gm? Ag100 Ol100.

Still off by default, still local-only.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.9.5
item.description:
fire.9.5 — AI Quotas gets brand icons

The AI Quotas readout now shows each provider's logo next to its weekly usage, instead of cryptic two-letter codes:

  [Codex] 22%  [Claude] 11%  [Antigravity] 0%  [Ollama] 43%

  • Per-provider brand icons (from your installed CodexBar app).
  • Weekly usage percent, color-coded: orange under 20% left, red under 10%.
  • Dropped the standalone Gemini entry — Antigravity already covers Gemini.
  • New per-provider toggles in Settings → Advanced → AI Quotas.

Still off by default, still local-only.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.9.6
item.description:
fire.9.6 — AI Quotas shows what's left, not what's used

The menu-bar readout now shows how much of each provider's weekly quota you have remaining, instead of how much you have already used:

  [Codex] 78%  [Claude] 89%  [Antigravity] 100%  [Ollama] 57%

  • A high number now means plenty of headroom; a small red number means you are almost out.
  • Antigravity at full quota reads "100%" instead of a confusing "0%".
  • Color thresholds unchanged: orange under 20% left, red under 10%.
  • Now matches the dropdown, which already lists "X% left" per window.

Still off by default, still local-only.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.9.7
item.description:
fire.9.7 — AI Quotas sits left of the system icons

  • The AI Quotas readout now sits to the LEFT of the macOS system icons (Spotlight, Control Center, clock), tucked just inside Ice's control item — instead of being wedged in between them on the right.
  • You can now drag it elsewhere and it stays put. Earlier builds snapped it back to the far right on every launch; only an off-screen position now self-heals.
  • Fixed an intermittent "?" for Codex caused by a stdout read race when shelling out to the CodexBar CLI.

Still off by default, still local-only.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.9.8
item.description:
fire.9.8 — AI menu-bar changes now ask first

When an AI assistant (via the MCP server) asks Fire to move, hide, or show a menu bar item, Fire now asks you to approve it first — using its own prompt, since Fire is the app that actually holds Accessibility permission:

  Deny  ·  Allow Once  ·  Allow for 5 Minutes

  • "Allow for 5 Minutes" lets a burst of changes (like applying a saved layout) through without re-prompting.
  • Closes a confused-deputy gap: previously any local process could trigger a privileged menu-bar change by dropping a command file. Now the file channel is just a request queue — approval happens in Fire.
  • Channel hardening: owner-only files, tighter staleness window, symlink/cross-user guards.

MCP server is still off by default and local-only.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.9.9
item.description:
fire.9.9 — no more freezes when the hidden bar appears

Fixes an issue where Fire could freeze for a couple of seconds (a brief "beachball") whenever the IceBar — the bar that shows your hidden menu bar items — appeared, moved, or refreshed.

  • The IceBar tints itself to match your desktop by capturing the screen behind it. That capture was running on the main thread; on macOS 26 it could block for seconds and freeze the whole app.
  • Now the capture runs off the main thread, so Fire stays responsive.
  • Found and confirmed via Sentry crash/hang reports — thank you to everyone with diagnostics enabled.

No feature changes — just smoother.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.10.0
item.description:
fire.10.0 — AI-Native Automations

Ask an AI assistant connected to Fire (via MCP) to set up a menu-bar automation, approve it once, and it runs on its own.

  • Automations rearrange your menu bar for you when something happens: an app comes to the front, your battery runs low, or a weekly time window begins.
  • You approve each automation once, in a Fire prompt that spells out exactly what it will do. Approval is bound to those exact items and that destination — change anything and Fire asks again.
  • Manage everything in the new Settings ​▸ Automations tab: enable/disable, see when each last ran, delete with a confirm, or disable all at once.
  • Created through three new MCP tools — set_trigger, list_triggers, remove_trigger — so any MCP client can wire them up.

Built on a hardened security model: a trigger is a sealed, user-approved capability, never a stored command, and every menu-bar change flows through one coordinator.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.10.1
item.description:
fire.10.1 — the freeze fix, finished

fire.9.9 promised an end to the brief freezes when the hidden-items bar appears. That fix turned out to be incomplete — it moved the screen capture off the main thread, but on macOS 26 the expensive pixel fetch still happened later, on the main thread, when the captured image was first used. Crash reports (thank you, diagnostics senders!) showed the hang was still occurring on fire.10.0.

  • The captured image is now fully rasterized on a background thread before the app ever touches it.
  • If that ever fails, Fire keeps the previous good image instead of risking a freeze.

No feature changes. If you saw a beachball when the bar popped up — this is the one.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.10.2
item.description:
fire.10.2 — audit pack: faster, leaner, harder to fool

A full code audit of the fork, shipped as one release.

  • Two more freeze classes fixed. Checking for AI-assistant requests used to touch the disk on the main thread five times a second — under disk pressure that could briefly freeze the app (one user hit it; thank you, diagnostics senders). That whole mechanism is gone. Also, a stuck usage-CLI can no longer freeze the AI Quotas item forever.
  • Internal messaging hardened. AI-assistant requests now travel over authenticated system messaging (XPC, same-developer peer check) instead of files on disk. Other software on your Mac can no longer inject menu-bar commands or fake their results. Consent prompts stay exactly as they were — now as a second line of defense. Bonus: two consent prompts can no longer appear on top of each other.
  • Automations re-prove their approval at the moment they run, not just when scheduled.
  • Leaner: over 700 lines of dead code removed; the IceBar color-matching pipeline now does all of its image work off the main thread.
  • Docs refreshed: all 10 MCP tools (including automations) are now documented.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.10.3
item.description:
fire.10.3 — safer automations, under the hood

A foundation + safety release. No new features — this is about making the AI automation surface trustworthy and the app's internals tested.

  • Automations stop drifting. A recurring multi-day schedule could silently stop working after a restart; fixed.
  • The approval prompt can't be faked. When an AI assistant proposes a menu-bar change, the name and app id it provides are now strictly validated, so they can't sneak hidden or misleading text into what you're approving.
  • Automations remember their cooldown across restarts and no longer re-fire on every login.
  • First-ever automated test suite for the security-critical logic, and crash/freeze reports now point at the exact code (developer symbols), so future issues get fixed faster.

If you'd created any automations, you'll be asked to approve them once more after updating (the approval format changed) — one click in Settings → Automations.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.10.4
item.description:
fire.10.4 — your settings tell the truth, and fewer freezes

A trust + stability release.

  • The MCP switches now actually do something. "Enable MCP server" and "Allow write operations" in Settings → Advanced used to be decorative — the server answered either way. Now they're real: turn the server off and AI assistants can't reach your menu bar at all; leave writes off and they can look but not touch.
  • Fewer freezes when revealing hidden icons. Clicking to expand a hidden item could briefly hang the app while it asked the window server which space each icon was on — on the main thread, and worse while another menu bar manager was running. That query now runs off the main thread.
  • Cleaner freeze reports. Harmless "waiting on a dialog" reports (like the "you're up to date" box left open) no longer drown out real freezes.

Existing setups are unchanged — if you were using the MCP integration it keeps working. New installs start with it off (opt-in).

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.10.4.1
item.description:
fire.10.4.1 — fewer freezes

A stability hotfix. fire.10.4's developer symbols pinpointed exactly where the app could briefly freeze on macOS 26, and this release moves that work off the main thread.

  • No more short freezes while revealing or rearranging menu bar icons. Several spots asked the window server for icon positions and on-screen window counts on the main thread; under load — especially with another menu bar manager also running — that could lock the UI for a few seconds. Those queries now run in the background.

Full release notes on GitHub

item.title: 0.11.13-fire.10.4.2
item.description:
fire.10.4.2 — fewer freezes

A small stability hotfix. Fire's event handling used to ask the window server whether its event tap was still active on every event it saw — including mouse moves. Under load (especially with another menu bar manager also running) that could briefly lock the UI. It now tracks that state locally, with no window-server round trip.
